name: Physics
description: Understand the laws that govern our universe

modules.title: Mechanics
modules.description: Motion, forces, and energy — the foundation of all physics

chapters.title: Motion & Speed
chapters.summary: Learn how to describe and measure how things move, from speed and velocity to distance-time graphs.

lessons.title: Speed and velocity
lessons.subtitle: How fast? And in which direction?

data.markdown: **Speed** tells you how fast something is moving — but not where it's going. **Velocity** tells you both the speed AND the direction. This difference matters in physics: a car going 60 km/h north and a car going 60 km/h south have the same speed but opposite velocities.

data.label: Speed formula — measured in m/s or km/h

steps.title: Speed is a scalar
steps.body: It has only magnitude (size). Example: 30 m/s

steps.title: Velocity is a vector
steps.body: It has both magnitude and direction. Example: 30 m/s heading east

steps.title: Average vs instantaneous
steps.body: Average speed = total distance ÷ total time. Your speedometer shows instantaneous speed.

data.question: A train travels 180 km in 3 hours. What is its average speed?
Velocity and speed always have the same value.
data.hint: Think about what happens when direction changes.
data.explanation: They are equal only when moving in a straight line without changing direction. Velocity can be negative; speed cannot.

data.markdown: **Acceleration** is the rate at which velocity changes over time. It can mean speeding up, slowing down (deceleration), or changing direction. Even a car turning at constant speed is accelerating — because its direction changes.

data.label: a = acceleration (m/s²),  v = final velocity,  u = initial velocity,  t = time

data.question: A car goes from 10 m/s to 40 m/s in 6 seconds. What is its acceleration?

config.label: Graph the relationship between distance, velocity & time

chapters.title: Forces & Newton's Laws
chapters.summary: Understand what forces are and how Newton's three laws explain the motion of everything around us.

data.markdown: Isaac Newton gave us three laws that describe how forces and motion relate. These laws explain why you stay in your seat on a bus, why rockets launch, and why objects fall to Earth. They work for everything from atoms to galaxies.

steps.title: First Law — Inertia
steps.body: An object stays at rest or keeps moving at constant velocity unless a net force acts on it. A book on a table stays put. A ball rolling on frictionless ice keeps rolling forever.

steps.title: Second Law — F = ma
steps.body: The net force on an object equals its mass × acceleration. Bigger force = more acceleration. Heavier object = less acceleration for same force.

steps.title: Third Law — Action-Reaction
steps.body: For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. You push down on the floor; the floor pushes up on you with the same force.

data.label: Newton's Second Law: Force (N) = mass (kg) × acceleration (m/s²)

config.label: Adjust forces to see Newton's Second Law in action

data.question: A 10 kg box is pushed with a net force of 30 N. What is its acceleration?

config.label: A pendulum — gravity + inertia in action

data.question: According to Newton's First Law, a moving object will eventually stop even without any friction.
data.hint: What does Newton's First Law say about objects in motion?
data.explanation: Without any force (including friction), a moving object keeps moving forever at constant velocity. It's friction that stops things in real life.
